title: Pediatric Asthma CONTROL and Self-Management Education in a 9-Year-Old with Poorly Controlled Persistent Asthma: Assessing Control, the CONTROLLER (Daily Inhaled Corticosteroid) vs RELIEVER (PRN Albuterol) Distinction, Correct Inhaler-and-Spacer Technique (and Rinsing After ICS), Peak-Flow Zones and a Written ASTHMA ACTION PLAN, Trigger Reduction, and When to Seek Care
subtitle: ASTHMA is a chronic INFLAMMATORY airway disease in which airway inflammation, bronchoconstriction, edema, and mucus produce recurrent WHEEZE, COUGH (often worse at NIGHT/early morning), chest tightness, and dyspnea, provoked by TRIGGERS (viral URIs — the most common pediatric trigger; allergens such as dust mites, pets, pollen, mold; TOBACCO/secondhand SMOKE; exercise; cold air; strong odors; GERD; and stress). This case is about CHRONIC CONTROL and SELF-MANAGEMENT (distinct from the acute, life-threatening status asthmaticus emergency). The GOAL of management is ASTHMA CONTROL: minimal/no daytime symptoms, NO nighttime awakenings, reliever use no more than ~2 days/week, NO activity limitation (a child with controlled asthma should play and exercise normally), normal lung function, and prevention of exacerbations. ASSESSING CONTROL drives therapy: a child is NOT well controlled if there are daytime symptoms or reliever (SABA) use MORE than ~2 days/week, ANY frequent nighttime awakenings, activity limitation, reduced peak flow/spirometry, or recurrent exacerbations/ED visits — findings that warrant STEPPING UP therapy and reviewing technique/adherence/triggers FIRST. The CENTRAL teaching distinction is CONTROLLER vs RELIEVER: the CONTROLLER (long-term, anti-inflammatory) medication — an INHALED CORTICOSTEROID (ICS, e.g., fluticasone, budesonide) — is taken EVERY DAY, even when the child feels well, to reduce airway inflammation and prevent attacks; it does NOT relieve an acute attack and takes weeks for full effect. Step-up options add a long-acting beta-agonist (ICS-LABA), a leukotriene modifier (montelukast), or higher-dose ICS. The RELIEVER/RESCUE medication — a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA, ALBUTEROL) — is used PRN for acute symptoms and before exercise and works within minutes. OVERUSE of the reliever (e.g., needing it more than twice a week, or going through canisters quickly) signals POOR CONTROL and undertreated inflammation — not a reason to simply use more albuterol. COMMON, DANGEROUS ERRORS to correct: relying ONLY on the reliever while SKIPPING the daily controller; trying to use the CONTROLLER to stop an acute attack (it won't work fast); and not understanding which inhaler is which. INHALER + SPACER technique is high-yield nursing teaching: use a SPACER (valved holding chamber) with a metered-dose inhaler to improve lung delivery and reduce oropharyngeal deposition (add a MASK for younger children) — shake, attach the spacer, exhale, seal the lips, actuate ONE puff, take a slow deep breath (or several tidal breaths with a mask), HOLD the breath ~10 seconds, and wait ~30-60 seconds between puffs; after using an INHALED CORTICOSTEROID, RINSE the mouth and SPIT (and clean the spacer) to prevent oral CANDIDIASIS (thrush) and hoarseness; track doses with the dose counter and prime/clean the device. PEAK-FLOW MONITORING and a WRITTEN ASTHMA ACTION PLAN guide self-management: establish the child's PERSONAL BEST, then use ZONES — GREEN (≥80% of personal best = good control, continue the controller), YELLOW (50-79% = caution, take the reliever and step up per the plan, watch closely), and RED (<50% = medical alert, take the reliever and seek emergency care) — and give the family and SCHOOL a written green/yellow/red action plan. TRIGGER REDUCTION and PREVENTION: maintain a SMOKE-FREE environment (eliminating secondhand smoke is the strongest modifiable factor), reduce dust-mite/allergen/pet exposure, treat allergic rhinitis/GERD, PRE-TREAT exercise-induced symptoms with a reliever rather than avoiding activity, get the annual INFLUENZA vaccine (and stay current on immunizations), and recognize and avoid the child's specific triggers. WHEN TO SEEK CARE / ADHERENCE / SCHOOL: teach the family to recognize WORSENING (increasing reliever use, night symptoms, yellow/red peak flow, fast or hard breathing, retractions, inability to speak in full sentences) and to follow the action plan/seek emergency care; support daily controller ADHERENCE with routines; ensure SCHOOL access to the reliever and a copy of the action plan; and arrange follow-up. NURSING ROLE: assess asthma control; teach the controller-vs-reliever distinction and correct inhaler/spacer technique (with mouth-rinsing after ICS); establish peak-flow zones and a written action plan; promote trigger reduction, immunization, and normal activity; teach recognition of worsening and when to seek care; and support adherence, school management, and follow-up.

patient.name: Mateo R.
patient.code: Outpatient pediatric care; goal = achieve asthma control through correct medication use, proper inhaler technique, a written action plan, trigger reduction, and family/school self-management education
patient.admitDate: A 9-year-old boy with persistent asthma is seen in the pediatric clinic for a follow-up after an emergency-department visit for an exacerbation last month. His mother reports he coughs and wheezes most days, wakes at night several times a week, and uses his albuterol inhaler about 5 days a week; he sits out of gym/soccer because of symptoms. He has a daily inhaled-corticosteroid controller prescribed but takes it only 'when he feels bad,' uses his metered-dose inhaler without a spacer and with poor coordination, and does not rinse his mouth afterward. There is secondhand smoke in the home and a cat. His asthma is NOT well controlled. This visit focuses on assessing control and delivering comprehensive self-management education (controller vs reliever, inhaler/spacer technique, peak-flow zones, a written action plan, trigger reduction, and when to seek care).
patient.room: Pediatric outpatient clinic; interdisciplinary team (pediatric provider, clinic RN, respiratory therapy/asthma educator, pharmacy, school nurse coordination); spirometry/peak-flow available; written asthma action plan and family/school teaching in progress

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; mild end-expiratory wheeze; by history daytime symptoms most days, nighttime awakenings several times/week, albuterol use ~5 days/week, activity limitation (sits out of sports), and an ED visit last month — asthma is NOT well controlled (warrants step-up + review of technique/adherence/triggers)

37.0°C; he uses the ALBUTEROL reliever for everything and takes the daily INHALED-CORTICOSTEROID controller only 'when he feels bad' — a key misunderstanding; the controller must be taken DAILY to prevent inflammation, and frequent reliever use signals poor control (not a cue to simply use more albuterol)

37.0°C; observed technique is poor — MDI used WITHOUT a spacer, poor breath coordination, and no mouth-rinsing after the steroid inhaler; teaching: use a SPACER, one puff with a slow deep breath and ~10-sec hold, wait between puffs, and RINSE/SPIT after the ICS to prevent thrush

37.0°C; established personal-best peak flow and a written ASTHMA ACTION PLAN — GREEN (≥80% = good, continue controller), YELLOW (50-79% = reliever + step up per plan), RED (<50% = reliever + seek emergency care); copies given to family and SCHOOL

37.0°C; reviewed trigger reduction — SMOKE-FREE home (eliminate secondhand smoke, the strongest modifiable factor), dust-mite/cat-allergen measures, treat allergic rhinitis, PRE-TREAT exercise with the reliever (keep playing sports), annual INFLUENZA vaccine, and recognizing personal triggers

37.0°C; with daily controller adherence, correct spacer technique, trigger reduction, and the action plan: symptoms minimal, no nighttime awakenings, rare reliever use, back to playing sports, peak flow in the green zone — asthma now well controlled

labs.v: Mateo R., 9, with persistent asthma, is seen for follow-up after an ED visit for an exacerbation. ASTHMA is a chronic INFLAMMATORY airway disease causing recurrent wheeze, cough (worse at night/early morning), chest tightness, and dyspnea, triggered by viral URIs (the most common pediatric trigger), allergens (dust mites, pets, pollen, mold), TOBACCO/secondhand SMOKE, exercise, cold air, odors, GERD, and stress. The GOAL is ASTHMA CONTROL: minimal/no daytime symptoms, NO nighttime awakenings, reliever use ≤2 days/week, NO activity limitation (the child should exercise/play normally), normal lung function, and prevention of exacerbations. ASSESSING CONTROL guides therapy: Mateo is NOT well controlled — daytime symptoms most days, nighttime awakenings several times/week, albuterol use ~5 days/week, activity limitation (sitting out of sports), and a recent ED visit. NOT-well-controlled asthma warrants STEPPING UP therapy AND first reviewing INHALER TECHNIQUE, ADHERENCE, and TRIGGER exposure (frequently the real problem). The nurse's central role here is to assess control accurately and then deliver comprehensive self-management education to move him toward control.
labs.r: Asthma = chronic INFLAMMATORY airway disease (wheeze/cough worse at night, chest tightness, dyspnea); triggers = viral URI (most common in kids), allergens, SMOKE, exercise, cold, GERD. GOAL = CONTROL: minimal symptoms, NO night awakenings, reliever ≤2 days/wk, NO activity limit, normal lung function, no exacerbations. NOT controlled if symptoms/SABA >2 days/wk, night symptoms, activity limit, low peak flow, ED visits → STEP UP + review technique/adherence/triggers. RN role: assess control.

labs.n: CONTROLLER (daily ICS) vs RELIEVER (PRN albuterol) — the central distinction
labs.v: The most important teaching point is the difference between the two kinds of asthma medicine. The CONTROLLER (long-term-control, anti-inflammatory) medication — an INHALED CORTICOSTEROID (ICS, e.g., fluticasone, budesonide) — is taken EVERY DAY, even when the child feels completely well, to reduce the underlying airway INFLAMMATION and PREVENT attacks; it works over days to weeks and does NOT relieve an acute attack. When control is inadequate, therapy is STEPPED UP (add a long-acting beta-agonist as an ICS-LABA combination, add a leukotriene modifier such as montelukast, or increase the ICS dose) — and stepped down once stable. The RELIEVER/RESCUE medication — a short-acting beta-agonist (SABA, ALBUTEROL) — is used as needed for acute symptoms and BEFORE EXERCISE, and works within minutes by relaxing bronchial smooth muscle. CRUCIALLY, frequent reliever use (more than ~2 days/week, or going through albuterol canisters quickly) is a RED FLAG for POOR CONTROL and undertreated inflammation — the correct response is to optimize the controller, technique, and triggers, NOT simply to use more albuterol. COMMON ERRORS the nurse corrects: using ONLY the reliever and SKIPPING the daily controller (Mateo's pattern); trying to use the controller to abort an acute attack (it is too slow); and confusing which inhaler is which. Clear labeling ('every-day medicine' vs 'rescue medicine') and teach-back help the child and family use them correctly.
labs.r: CONTROLLER = INHALED CORTICOSTEROID, taken DAILY even when well, anti-inflammatory, PREVENTS attacks, NOT for acute relief (step up: ICS-LABA, montelukast, higher ICS). RELIEVER = SABA (ALBUTEROL), PRN for acute symptoms + before exercise, works in minutes. Frequent reliever use (>2 days/wk) = POOR CONTROL → optimize controller/technique/triggers, NOT just more albuterol. ERRORS: reliever-only/skipping controller; using controller for acute attack; confusing the two. RN role: teach controller-vs-reliever.

labs.n: Inhaler + spacer technique + rinsing after ICS
labs.v: Correct device technique is essential — many 'uncontrolled' children simply use their inhaler incorrectly. Use a SPACER (valved holding chamber) with a metered-dose inhaler: it improves medication delivery to the lungs and reduces deposition in the mouth/throat; younger children add a MASK. STEPS: remove the cap and SHAKE the inhaler; attach it to the spacer; have the child EXHALE fully; seal the lips around the spacer mouthpiece; actuate ONE puff into the spacer; take a SLOW, DEEP breath in (or, with a mask, breathe normally for several breaths); HOLD the breath for ~10 seconds; and WAIT about 30-60 seconds before a second puff (repeat the steps for each puff). After using an INHALED CORTICOSTEROID, the child should RINSE the mouth with water and SPIT it out (and the spacer should be cleaned periodically) to PREVENT oral CANDIDIASIS (thrush) and hoarseness — a key, frequently-missed step. Teach families to track doses with the device's DOSE COUNTER (so the inhaler is not used past empty), to PRIME and CLEAN the device per instructions, and to store it properly. The nurse uses demonstration and TEACH-BACK (have the child show the technique) and reassesses at each visit, because technique drifts. Mateo was using no spacer, poor coordination, and not rinsing — all corrected with teaching.
labs.r: Use a SPACER with the MDI (mask for young kids) — improves lung delivery, reduces oral deposition. Technique: shake → attach spacer → exhale → seal lips → ONE puff → slow deep breath → HOLD ~10 sec → wait 30-60 sec between puffs. After ICS: RINSE mouth + SPIT (prevent thrush/hoarseness); clean spacer. Track doses (counter), prime/clean. Use demonstration + TEACH-BACK + reassess (technique drifts). RN role: teach + verify inhaler/spacer technique.

labs.n: Peak-flow zones + written ASTHMA ACTION PLAN + when to seek care
labs.v: Self-management is structured by PEAK-FLOW monitoring and a WRITTEN ASTHMA ACTION PLAN. A peak-flow meter measures how fast the child can exhale; first establish the child's PERSONAL BEST (highest of readings when well), then interpret using ZONES based on percent of personal best: GREEN ZONE (≥80% = good control — continue the daily controller and usual activities); YELLOW ZONE (50-79% = CAUTION — an exacerbation may be starting; take the reliever and follow the step-up instructions on the plan, and monitor closely); and RED ZONE (<50% = MEDICAL ALERT — take the reliever and SEEK EMERGENCY CARE). The provider creates an individualized WRITTEN ACTION PLAN in green/yellow/red format specifying the daily controller, the reliever dose, what to do in each zone, and when to call or go to the ED, and copies are given to the FAMILY and the SCHOOL. Teach the family to recognize WORSENING regardless of the meter: increasing reliever use, nighttime symptoms, fast or labored breathing, retractions or nasal flaring, difficulty speaking in full sentences, or a poor response to the reliever — these mean follow the yellow/red plan and seek care. The action plan turns symptoms and numbers into clear, pre-decided actions, reducing delays and ED visits.
labs.r: PEAK FLOW: establish PERSONAL BEST, then zones — GREEN ≥80% (good, continue controller), YELLOW 50-79% (caution, reliever + step up per plan), RED <50% (emergency, reliever + seek care). Provider writes an individualized green/yellow/red WRITTEN ASTHMA ACTION PLAN → copies to FAMILY + SCHOOL. Seek care for: rising reliever use, night symptoms, fast/labored breathing, retractions, can't speak full sentences, poor reliever response. RN role: peak-flow zones + action plan + recognize worsening.

labs.n: Trigger reduction + prevention + adherence/school; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: TRIGGER REDUCTION and PREVENTION reduce exacerbations: keep a SMOKE-FREE home and car (eliminating SECONDHAND SMOKE is the single strongest modifiable factor — counsel/refer for tobacco cessation), reduce DUST-MITE exposure (allergen-proof mattress/pillow covers, wash bedding in hot water, reduce stuffed toys/carpet), manage PET allergens (keep the cat out of the bedroom or rehome if severe), treat ALLERGIC RHINITIS and GERD, and identify/avoid the child's specific triggers. Encourage NORMAL ACTIVITY: a child with controlled asthma should play and exercise — for exercise-induced symptoms, PRE-TREAT with the reliever ~15 minutes before activity and warm up, rather than avoiding sports. PREVENTIVE care: the annual INFLUENZA vaccine and keeping immunizations current. ADHERENCE and SCHOOL: support a daily controller ROUTINE (link to brushing teeth, reminders/charts), ensure the SCHOOL has the reliever available and a copy of the action plan, and arrange FOLLOW-UP to reassess control and step therapy up or down. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct practice): the nurse did NOT tell the family to just use more albuterol for frequent symptoms (a sign to optimize the controller/technique/triggers); did NOT advise stopping the daily controller when feeling well; did NOT recommend avoiding all exercise; did NOT skip the spacer or the post-ICS mouth rinse; and did NOT overlook the secondhand smoke. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: accurate control assessment; correct controller-vs-reliever use; proper inhaler/spacer technique with mouth-rinsing; peak-flow zones and a written action plan shared with school; trigger reduction (especially smoke-free) and immunization; normal activity with exercise pre-treatment; and adherence support with follow-up. EXPECTED outcome: symptoms and reliever use decrease, nighttime awakenings stop, activity is normal, peak flow stays green, and exacerbations/ED visits fall. NURSING ROLE: assess control; teach controller-vs-reliever and inhaler/spacer technique (rinse after ICS); establish peak-flow zones and a written action plan; promote trigger reduction, immunization, and normal activity; teach recognition of worsening/when to seek care; and support adherence, school management, and follow-up.
labs.r: TRIGGER reduction: SMOKE-FREE (strongest modifiable — cessation referral), dust-mite/pet-allergen measures, treat rhinitis/GERD, avoid personal triggers. NORMAL activity — PRE-TREAT exercise with reliever (don't avoid sports). Annual FLU vaccine + immunizations. ADHERENCE routine + SCHOOL has reliever + action-plan copy + follow-up. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't say 'just use more albuterol,' didn't stop controller when well, didn't avoid exercise, didn't skip spacer/post-ICS rinse, didn't ignore secondhand smoke. PROTECTIVE: control assessment, correct med use/technique, action plan, trigger reduction, immunization, adherence/follow-up. RN role: prevention + adherence + school + follow-up.

nursesNote: Pediatric clinic — Mateo R., 9, persistent asthma, follow-up after an ED exacerbation last month. NOT well controlled: daytime symptoms most days, nighttime awakenings several times/wk, albuterol ~5 days/wk, sits out of sports (activity limitation). Asthma = chronic INFLAMMATORY airway disease (wheeze/cough worse at night/AM, chest tightness, dyspnea); triggers = viral URI (#1 in kids), allergens (dust mites/cat), SECONDHAND SMOKE (home), exercise, cold, GERD. GOAL = CONTROL (minimal symptoms, no night awakenings, reliever ≤2 days/wk, NO activity limit, normal lung function, no exacerbations). Not controlled → STEP UP + review technique/adherence/triggers FIRST. CENTRAL TEACHING — CONTROLLER vs RELIEVER: CONTROLLER = daily INHALED CORTICOSTEROID (fluticasone/budesonide), taken EVERY DAY even when well (anti-inflammatory, PREVENTS attacks, NOT fast relief; step up = ICS-LABA/montelukast/higher ICS); RELIEVER = SABA ALBUTEROL PRN for acute symptoms + before exercise (works in minutes). Frequent reliever use (>2 days/wk) = POOR CONTROL → optimize controller/technique/triggers, NOT just more albuterol. Mateo's errors: uses albuterol for everything + takes ICS only 'when he feels bad' (must be DAILY); MDI without spacer + poor coordination + no post-ICS rinse. INHALER/SPACER teaching: use a SPACER (mask for young kids); shake → attach → exhale → seal lips → ONE puff → slow deep breath → HOLD ~10 sec → wait 30-60 sec between puffs; after ICS RINSE mouth + SPIT (prevent thrush/hoarseness); clean spacer, track doses, prime; demonstration + TEACH-BACK + reassess. PEAK FLOW: personal best → GREEN ≥80% (continue controller), YELLOW 50-79% (reliever + step up per plan), RED <50% (reliever + EMERGENCY care). WRITTEN ASTHMA ACTION PLAN (green/yellow/red) → copies to FAMILY + SCHOOL. Seek care: rising reliever use, night symptoms, fast/labored breathing, retractions/flaring, can't speak full sentences, poor reliever response. TRIGGER reduction: SMOKE-FREE home/car (strongest modifiable → cessation referral), dust-mite covers/wash bedding hot/reduce carpet+stuffed toys, cat out of bedroom, treat allergic rhinitis/GERD. NORMAL activity — PRE-TREAT exercise with reliever (keep playing). Annual FLU vaccine + immunizations. ADHERENCE routine + SCHOOL reliever access + action-plan copy + follow-up. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't say 'just use more albuterol,' didn't stop controller when well, didn't advise avoiding exercise, didn't skip spacer/post-ICS rinse, didn't ignore secondhand smoke. Outcome at follow-up: minimal symptoms, no night awakenings, rare reliever use, back to sports, peak flow green — controlled.
providerOrders: VISIT: pediatric clinic follow-up; persistent asthma, not well controlled (recent ED exacerbation)
ASSESSMENT: assess asthma control (daytime symptoms, nighttime awakenings, reliever frequency, activity limitation, exacerbations); spirometry/peak flow; review technique, adherence, and triggers

CONTROLLER: daily inhaled corticosteroid (e.g., fluticasone) — take EVERY day even when well; step up therapy (ICS-LABA, leukotriene modifier, or higher-dose ICS) for inadequate control; step down once stable

RELIEVER: short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) as needed for acute symptoms and 15 minutes before exercise; frequent use (>2 days/week) indicates poor control and should prompt controller/technique/trigger review

DEVICE TEACHING: use a spacer (with mask for younger children) with the MDI; teach shake/exhale/one puff/slow deep breath/10-second hold/wait between puffs; RINSE mouth and spit after the inhaled corticosteroid; verify with teach-back at each visit; track doses with the counter

PEAK FLOW + ACTION PLAN: establish personal best; provide an individualized written asthma action plan (green ≥80% continue controller; yellow 50-79% reliever + step up; red <50% reliever + emergency care); give copies to the family and school
TRIGGER REDUCTION: smoke-free home/car (counsel/refer for tobacco cessation); dust-mite and pet-allergen measures; treat allergic rhinitis/GERD; identify/avoid the child's triggers
PREVENTION/ACTIVITY: encourage normal activity with pre-exercise reliever; annual influenza vaccine and current immunizations

EDUCATION: recognize worsening (increasing reliever use, night symptoms, fast/labored breathing, retractions, inability to speak in full sentences) and follow the action plan/seek care; support daily controller adherence; coordinate school management
FOLLOW-UP: reassess control and adjust therapy (step up/down); reinforce technique and adherence

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for this child's asthma control?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Daytime symptoms most days, nighttime awakenings several times a week, albuterol use ~5 days/week, and activity limitation — signs of poorly controlled asthma
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — these define inadequate control and the need to step up and re-educate. RN role: assess control.

opts.text: He takes the daily inhaled-corticosteroid controller only 'when he feels bad' and uses the albuterol reliever for everything
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — a central, correctable misunderstanding of controller vs reliever. RN role: teach the medication distinction.

opts.text: His inhaler technique is poor — no spacer, poor coordination, and no mouth-rinsing after the steroid inhaler
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — poor technique is a common cause of 'uncontrolled' asthma and thrush risk. RN role: assess/teach technique.

opts.text: Frequent reliever use is a red flag for poor control, not a reason to simply use more albuterol
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — over-reliance on the reliever signals undertreated inflammation. RN role: recognize the red flag + optimize the controller.

opts.text: Modifiable triggers — secondhand smoke in the home and a cat — and the need for a peak-flow plan and trigger reduction
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — trigger reduction (especially smoke-free) is high-impact prevention. RN role: address triggers.

opts.text: The need for a written asthma action plan, normal activity with exercise pre-treatment, the flu vaccine, and school coordination
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — an action plan, activity, immunization, and school access support control. RN role: action plan + prevention + school.

opts.text: Allergies: environmental (dust mites, cat dander) documented
opts.rat: Relevant to trigger reduction; documented. Supports the plan rather than being the central cue.

opts.text: Advise the family to just increase albuterol use and stop the daily controller whenever he feels well
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Frequent reliever use signals poor control; the daily controller must continue even when well to prevent inflammation — stopping it worsens control.

opts.text: Tell him to avoid all sports and exercise permanently because he has asthma
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A child with controlled asthma should play and exercise; pre-treat exercise-induced symptoms with the reliever rather than avoiding activity.

opts.text: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease, and the goal is control — minimal symptoms, no nighttime awakenings, reliever use ≤2 days/week, and normal activity
opts.rat: CORRECT — disease nature and the control goal. Foundation for assessment.

opts.text: The controller (daily inhaled corticosteroid) is taken every day to prevent inflammation and does not relieve an acute attack
opts.rat: CORRECT — controller principle. Foundation for correct daily use.

opts.text: The reliever (albuterol) is for acute symptoms and before exercise; frequent use signals poor control
opts.rat: CORRECT — reliever principle and the overuse red flag. Foundation for recognizing inadequate control.

opts.text: A spacer improves delivery, and the mouth should be rinsed after an inhaled corticosteroid to prevent thrush
opts.rat: CORRECT — device technique and post-ICS rinsing. Foundation for effective, safe use.

opts.text: Peak-flow zones (green ≥80%, yellow 50-79%, red <50%) and a written action plan guide self-management
opts.rat: CORRECT — peak-flow/action-plan principle. Foundation for structured self-management.

opts.text: Reducing triggers (especially eliminating secondhand smoke), getting the flu vaccine, and pre-treating exercise support control and normal activity
opts.rat: CORRECT — trigger reduction and prevention. Foundation for fewer exacerbations.

opts.text: The inhaled corticosteroid should be used as a quick-relief medicine during an acute asthma attack
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The inhaled corticosteroid is a daily controller that works over time; it does not provide quick relief — the SABA reliever is used for acute symptoms.

opts.text: Needing the reliever many times a week is fine as long as it keeps working, and the controller can be skipped
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Frequent reliever need indicates poor control/undertreated inflammation; the controller must be continued and therapy/technique/triggers optimized.

opts.text: Assess asthma control and review inhaler technique, adherence, and trigger exposure
opts.rat: #1 — Accurate control assessment (and finding technique/adherence/trigger gaps) drives the whole plan. RN role: assess control + identify gaps.

opts.text: Teach the controller-vs-reliever distinction — daily inhaled corticosteroid to prevent, albuterol PRN to relieve
opts.rat: #2 — Correcting this central misunderstanding is the highest-impact education. RN role: teach medication roles.

opts.text: Teach and verify correct inhaler/spacer technique, including rinsing the mouth after the steroid inhaler
opts.rat: #3 — Proper technique makes the medications actually work and prevents thrush. RN role: device teaching + teach-back.

opts.text: Establish peak-flow zones and provide a written asthma action plan (with a school copy)
opts.rat: #4 — Structured self-management turns symptoms/numbers into pre-decided actions. RN role: action plan.

opts.text: Promote trigger reduction (smoke-free), immunization, normal activity with exercise pre-treatment, and follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Prevention and follow-up sustain control over time. RN role: trigger reduction + prevention + follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this child's asthma care.

opts.text: Teach that the daily inhaled corticosteroid is taken every day, even when well, to prevent inflammation
opts.rat: Correct controller use is the foundation of control. RN role: teach daily controller.

opts.text: Teach that albuterol is the reliever for acute symptoms and before exercise, and that frequent use means control should be optimized
opts.rat: Correct reliever use and the overuse red flag. RN role: teach reliever role.

opts.text: Provide a spacer and teach correct technique, including rinsing the mouth after the steroid inhaler
opts.rat: Improves delivery and prevents thrush. RN role: device technique + post-ICS rinse.

opts.text: Establish a personal-best peak flow and provide a green/yellow/red written action plan, with a copy for the school
opts.rat: Structured self-management with school access. RN role: peak flow + action plan.

opts.text: Counsel a smoke-free home, dust-mite/pet measures, the annual flu vaccine, and pre-treating exercise so he can keep playing sports
opts.rat: Trigger reduction, prevention, and normal activity. RN role: trigger reduction + prevention.

opts.text: Use demonstration and teach-back and arrange follow-up to reassess control and technique
opts.rat: Verifies learning and sustains control. RN role: teach-back + follow-up.

opts.text: Advise stopping the daily controller whenever he feels well and just using more albuterol for symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — stopping the controller worsens control, and frequent reliever use signals undertreatment; the controller continues and therapy is optimized.

opts.text: Tell him to use the inhaled corticosteroid as his quick-relief medicine during an attack
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the inhaled corticosteroid is a slow-acting daily controller, not a rescue medicine; the SABA reliever is used for acute symptoms.

opts.text: Recommend he avoid all sports and physical activity because of his asthma
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — controlled asthma allows normal activity; pre-treat exercise with the reliever rather than avoiding it.

opts.text: Tell the family that secondhand smoke in the home is unrelated to his asthma
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — secondhand smoke is a major modifiable trigger; a smoke-free environment is one of the most important interventions.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess asthma control and review current technique, adherence, and triggers
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Understanding control and the gaps directs all teaching. RN role: assess + identify gaps.

opts.text: Teach the controller-vs-reliever distinction and confirm the daily controller routine
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Correcting the central misunderstanding has the greatest impact. RN role: teach medication roles.

opts.text: Teach and verify inhaler/spacer technique (with mouth-rinsing after the steroid) using teach-back
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Proper technique makes the medications effective and prevents thrush. RN role: device teaching.

opts.text: Establish peak-flow zones and provide a written action plan with a school copy
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Structured self-management guides home/school response. RN role: action plan.

opts.text: Counsel trigger reduction, immunization, and normal activity, and arrange follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Prevention and follow-up sustain control. RN role: prevention + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: After daily controller use, correct spacer technique, and trigger reduction, his daytime symptoms and nighttime awakenings decrease and reliever use drops to rare
opts.rat: The hallmark of regained control with correct self-management. Expected, desired improvement.

opts.text: He returns to playing soccer, pre-treating with his reliever before exercise, with peak flow in the green zone
opts.rat: Normal activity and green-zone peak flow reflect good control. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: The family makes the home smoke-free and uses dust-mite covers, and his exacerbations and ED visits decrease
opts.rat: Trigger reduction lowering exacerbations. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: He demonstrates correct inhaler/spacer technique and rinses after the steroid inhaler, with no thrush
opts.rat: Effective technique and thrush prevention. Improving trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the family stops the daily controller when he feels well and relies on frequent albuterol; symptoms, night awakenings, and exacerbations increase
opts.rat: ⚠️ Skipping the controller and over-relying on the reliever worsens control. Demonstrates why the daily controller continues.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — he continues to use the inhaler without a spacer and never rinses after the steroid; he gets poor delivery and develops oral thrush
opts.rat: ⚠️ Poor technique reduces effectiveness and causes thrush. Demonstrates the importance of spacer use and post-ICS rinsing.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — secondhand smoke continues at home and the cat sleeps in his room; he has frequent nighttime symptoms and yellow-zone peak flows
opts.rat: ⚠️ Ongoing trigger exposure perpetuates poor control. Demonstrates the impact of trigger reduction (especially smoke-free).

opts.text: Alternative scenario — told to avoid all exercise, he becomes deconditioned and anxious while his asthma remains poorly controlled
opts.rat: ⚠️ Avoiding activity is incorrect and harmful; controlled asthma should allow exercise with pre-treatment. Demonstrates promoting normal activity.

opts.text: Right after the visit, before the controller takes full effect, his symptoms and peak flow are essentially unchanged while he begins daily use
opts.rat: The controller works over days to weeks; an unchanged short-term baseline is expected while adherence builds. Continue and reassess at follow-up.

opts.text: At follow-up he is well controlled, demonstrates his action plan, and the school has a copy and his reliever on hand
opts.rat: Sustained control with effective self-management and school coordination. Ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Health Promotion and Maintenance, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
